header.subtitle: Module 2: AI Memory | How Tools Learn About You
sec.problema.intro: Every conversation with an AI starts from scratch. Without memory, the model doesn't know who you are, what your project is, or which decisions have already been made. The result? Repetition, lost context, and generic answers.
sec.problema.sem.label: // Conversation 5, same project, same person
sec.problema.sem.user: You: I need to add authentication to the project
sec.problema.sem.ai: AI: Sure! What language do you use? What framework? Do you have a database? JWT or sessions? (asks everything again)
sec.problema.sem.waste: ~2,000 tokens spent rebuilding context that already existed
sec.problema.com.label: // Conversation 5, with active memory
sec.problema.com.user: You: I need to add authentication to the project
sec.problema.com.ai: AI: Based on your Node.js + Express project with PostgreSQL, I recommend JWT with refresh tokens. I'll follow the middleware pattern you already use in src/middleware/. (context preserved)
sec.problema.com.save: 0 tokens spent on re-context. Direct, personalized answer.
sec.problema.chart.title: Context tokens spent per conversation
sec.problema.chart.caption: Without memory, every conversation rebuilds context from scratch. With memory, the cost drops progressively.
sec.tipos.intro: Different tools implement memory in different ways. Understanding the types helps you choose the right approach.
sec.tipos.session.title: Session Memory
sec.tipos.session.desc: Exists only during the current conversation. This is the default in ChatGPT, Claude.ai, and all AI chats. When the conversation closes, the context disappears.
sec.tipos.user.title: User Memory
sec.tipos.user.desc: Persists across conversations. Stores preferences, professional role, and work patterns. ChatGPT Memory and Claude Code auto-memory use this type.
sec.tipos.project.title: Project Memory
sec.tipos.project.desc: Tied to a specific repository or workspace. Includes architecture decisions, known bugs, and project state. CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md are examples.
sec.tipos.instruction.title: Persistent Instructions
sec.tipos.instruction.desc: Configuration files that the AI reads automatically in every session. The most predictable form of "memory", directly controlled by the developer.
sec.tipos.insight: Insight: The most powerful combination is persistent instructions (predictable, controlled) + project memory (dynamic, learned). This is exactly what Claude Code does with CLAUDE.md + auto-memory.
sec.memorymd.intro: Claude Code has an automatic file-based memory system. Each project can have its own memory directory at .claude/memory/.
sec.memorymd.structure.label: # Memory directory structure
sec.memorymd.structure.index: # Index: pointers to each memory file
sec.memorymd.example.title: Example: MEMORY.md (the index)
sec.memorymd.file.title: Example: individual memory file
sec.memorymd.keypoint: Key point: MEMORY.md is just the index. Each memory lives in its own file with YAML frontmatter. Claude reads the index at the start of every conversation and accesses individual files as needed.
sec.quatrotipos.h2: The 4 Memory Types in Claude Code
sec.quatrotipos.intro: Claude Code organizes memories into 4 categories. Each type has a specific purpose and guides when and how to save information.
sec.quatrotipos.user.title: User Memory
sec.quatrotipos.user.when: When to save: when learning details about the user's role, preferences, or knowledge
sec.quatrotipos.user.desc: Information about who you are, what you do, and how you prefer to work. Allows Claude to adapt responses to your level and professional context. Claude creates a .md file in .claude/memory/ and adds an entry to the MEMORY.md index.
sec.quatrotipos.user.example.label: Trigger example:
sec.quatrotipos.user.example.trigger: "I'm a data scientist, investigating the system logs"
sec.quatrotipos.user.file.label: File created: user_role.md
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.title: Feedback Memory
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.when: When to save: when the user corrects OR confirms an approach
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.desc: Corrections and confirmations about how to work. The most important type: prevents the same mistake from repeating in future conversations. Creates a .md file with the rule, the reason (Why), and how to apply (How to apply).
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.wrong.label: Correction:
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.wrong.text: "Don't mock the database. We got burned last quarter when mocked tests passed but the migration failed in production"
sec.quatrotipos.feedback.file.label: File created: feedback_no_mocks.md
sec.quatrotipos.project.title: Project Memory
sec.quatrotipos.project.when: When to save: when learning about ongoing work, goals, decisions, or deadlines
sec.quatrotipos.project.desc: Information about the current state of the project that is not derivable from code or git history. Includes decisions, motivations, and deadlines. Creates a .md file with the fact, the reason (Why), and how to apply (How to apply). Relative dates are converted to absolute ones.
sec.quatrotipos.project.example.label: Trigger example:
sec.quatrotipos.project.example.trigger: "We're freezing non-critical merges after Thursday. Mobile team is cutting the release branch"
sec.quatrotipos.project.file.label: File created: project_merge_freeze.md
sec.quatrotipos.reference.title: Reference Memory
sec.quatrotipos.reference.when: When to save: when learning where information lives in external systems
sec.quatrotipos.reference.desc: Creates a .md file with pointers to external systems. Works like an address book: it doesn't store the content of the external system, only where to find it. Useful because the content changes frequently, but the address remains stable.
sec.quatrotipos.reference.example.label: Trigger example:
sec.quatrotipos.reference.example.trigge: "Pipeline bugs are tracked in Linear project 'INGEST'"
sec.quatrotipos.reference.file.label: File created: reference_linear_ingest.md
sec.pratica.intro: In Claude Code, memories can be saved explicitly or detected automatically. Here's how to use them in practice.
sec.pratica.step1.title: Explicit request
sec.pratica.step1.desc: Tell Claude to remember something and it automatically saves to the memory directory.
sec.pratica.step1.example: "Remember that this project uses PostgreSQL with Prisma and tests run against a local Docker container."
sec.pratica.step2.title: Auto-detection
sec.pratica.step2.desc: Claude detects corrections, confirmations, and important information during conversation and saves proactively.
sec.pratica.step2.example: "No, don't use npm. This project uses pnpm with workspaces."
sec.pratica.step2.result: → Saves feedback: "project uses pnpm with workspaces, not npm"
sec.pratica.step3.title: Review and edit
sec.pratica.step3.desc: Memories are regular Markdown files in .claude/memory/. You can edit, remove, or reorganize them at any time.
sec.pratica.step3.example: "What do you remember about this project?"
sec.pratica.step3.forget: "Forget the information about the API v3 deadline"
sec.pratica.step4.title: Automatic recall
sec.pratica.step4.desc: MEMORY.md is loaded automatically at the start of every conversation. Relevant memories are accessed as needed during the session.
sec.pratica.step4.note: Important: Claude checks if memories are still valid before acting on them. If a memory references a file that no longer exists, it updates or removes it automatically.
sec.pratica.whatnot.title: What NOT to save in memory
sec.pratica.whatnot.1: Code patterns or architecture (read from the code)
sec.pratica.whatnot.2: Git history or who changed what (use git log)
sec.pratica.whatnot.3: Debugging solutions (the fix is in the commit)
sec.limites.intro: AI memory is not magic. Understanding the limits prevents surprises and decisions based on stale information.
sec.limites.stale.title: Memories go stale
sec.limites.stale.desc: A memory that says "we use Express" may be wrong if the team migrated to Fastify. Memories are snapshots of a moment, not permanent truths. Claude tries to verify before acting, but can't always detect silent changes.
sec.limites.privacy.title: Be careful with sensitive information
sec.limites.privacy.desc: Memories are plaintext files in the repository. Never save passwords, tokens, API keys, or PII (personally identifiable information) as memory. Treat the memory directory like any other project file.
sec.limites.overreliance.title: Don't trust blindly
sec.limites.overreliance.desc: A memory says function X exists, but it may have been renamed or removed. Before recommending something based on memory, Claude should verify against the current code. "The memory says X exists" is not the same as "X exists now".
sec.limites.curate.title: Regular curation
sec.limites.curate.desc: Review memories periodically. Remove what's stale, update deadlines that have passed, and consolidate fragmented memories. A clean memory is better than a large one.
sec.limites.gitignore.title: Consider .gitignore
sec.limites.gitignore.desc: If the memory directory contains personal or workflow-specific information, consider adding .claude/memory/ to .gitignore. Project memory can be committed; personal memory usually shouldn't.
sec.avancado.intro: The flat pattern (everything in .claude/memory/) works for small projects. But when memories grow, MEMORY.md exceeds the 200-line limit and gets truncated. Claude loses access to some memories. The solution: subfolders by category with Skill + Agent to maintain the convention.
sec.avancado.how.title: The 3 required pieces
sec.avancado.how.intro: Claude Code doesn't create subfolders on its own: it needs explicit instructions. The combination of CLAUDE.md + Skill + Agent solves this.
sec.avancado.piece1.title: CLAUDE.md: the base instruction
sec.avancado.piece1.desc: Add the rule for saving memories to your project's CLAUDE.md. This is read in every conversation.
sec.avancado.piece2.title: Skill: the save command
sec.avancado.piece2.desc: A skill in .claude/commands/save-memory.md that Claude invokes to create the file in the right place.
sec.avancado.piece3.title: Agent: the automatic curator
sec.avancado.piece3.desc: An agent in .claude/agents/memory-curator.md that reviews, cleans, and reorganizes memories periodically.
sec.avancado.why.title: Why it works
sec.avancado.why.1: Lightweight MEMORY.md: Only pointers to folders (~10 lines). Never hits the 200-line limit.
sec.avancado.why.2: On-demand reading: Claude only opens the README.md of the relevant folder, doesn't load all memories.
sec.avancado.why.3: Scalable: 100+ memories organized in ~8 folders, each with its own index.
sec.avancado.why.4: Automatic curation: The agent reviews periodically without manual intervention.
sec.avancado.note: Note: This pattern is a manual convention. Claude Code doesn't create subfolders by default. You need all 3 pieces (CLAUDE.md instruction + skill + agent) for Claude to follow the convention. Without them, it falls back to default flat behavior.
sec.proximos.intro: Four steps to activate and use AI memory in your project.
sec.proximos.step1.title: Check if memory is active
sec.proximos.step1.desc: In Claude Code, check if the .claude/memory/ directory exists in your project. If not, just ask: "Remember that this is a Node.js project with TypeScript".
sec.proximos.step2.title: Start with 2-3 fundamental memories
sec.proximos.step2.desc: Save your role (user), a strong team preference (feedback), and a current project context (project). Don't try to save everything at once.
sec.proximos.step3.title: Observe auto-detection
sec.proximos.step3.desc: In upcoming conversations, watch when Claude saves memories on its own. Review MEMORY.md after a few sessions to check quality.
sec.proximos.step4.title: Establish a curation cycle
sec.proximos.step4.desc: Once a week, open MEMORY.md and ask: is each memory here still true? Remove what's stale, update what changed.
sec.proximos.mantra.label: The Part 2 Mantra:
sec.proximos.mantra.text: "AI memory is only as good as the human curation behind it."
sec.proximos.refs.title: References & Next Module
